title: Evolutionary Data Through Schemaboi: Achieving Forward, Backwards, and Sideways Compatibility
gist: At a local-first software conference, Seph Gentle argued durable interoperable applications need a self-describing schema embedded in the data file itself (unlike Protocol Buffers, Cap'n Proto, or JSON), demonstrating the idea with an experimental Rust format called schemaboi that preserves unknown fields and introduces 'sideways compatibility' for independent, uncoordinated schema evolution.

themes.theme: The case for self-describing, durable schemas

insights.claim: Seph Gentle's central design thesis is that a durable, interoperable data format for local-first applications must be self-describing, statically typed, preserve foreign/unknown data it doesn't understand, and remain compatible over long time horizons -- criteria he argues Protocol Buffers and Cap'n Proto (pre-shared schemas) and JSON (verbose, weakly typed) all fail to meet.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: A direct critique of the dominant serialization formats used across distributed systems and APIs today, relevant to any team evaluating long-lived data formats for local-first or interoperable applications.
insights.locator: A central theme of the talk was the necessity of a durable schema format

insights.claim: Gentle's proposed approach embeds the schema directly into the initial bytes of every file so any tool can inspect and interpret data without a separately distributed schema file, demonstrated in an experimental Rust implementation called schemaboi where missing fields get filled with defaults and unknown fields are preserved (not discarded) across app versions.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: A concrete engineering pattern -- in-file schema plus field preservation -- for maintaining data integrity across independently-evolving software versions without centralized schema coordination.
insights.locator: To achieve these goals, Gentle introduced the concept

themes.theme: A new compatibility category: sideways compatibility

insights.claim: Beyond standard backward and forward compatibility, Gentle introduced 'sideways compatibility' -- the ability for disparate developers to add fields to a shared data format independently without central coordination -- enabled by using globally unique field identifiers instead of simple incrementing tags to prevent collisions during parallel development.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Names and formalizes a compatibility requirement (uncoordinated multi-developer schema extension) that is increasingly relevant as local-first and multi-vendor interoperable app ecosystems grow -- a concept other schema/protocol designers could adopt.
insights.locator: Gentle also established clear definitions for different types of compatibility

insights.claim: Gentle's safety guidance for schema evolution: adding optional fields or new enum variants is generally safe, while making a field mandatory, renaming fields, or changing a field's type are flagged as high-risk actions that should be avoided in favor of incremental, additive changes -- citing email and HTTP as long-lived systems that succeeded through evolution rather than forced migration.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: A practical, actionable governance rule set for any team designing or maintaining a long-lived data schema or API contract.
insights.locator: The talk included specific safety guidelines for schema evolution

insights.claim: Benchmarks show schemaboi is significantly smaller and faster than JSON, but Gentle explicitly cautions the project remains experimental and not production-ready.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Signals schemaboi is a research prototype worth tracking rather than an immediately adoptable production format.
insights.locator: While benchmarks showed that his format is significantly smaller and faster than JSON

insights.claim: Gentle's broader vision is a future where user data is truly owned by the user, independent of the specific applications that created it -- positioning durable self-describing schemas as foundational infrastructure for a local-first, app-independent data layer.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Connects the technical schema-design work to the broader local-first/data-sovereignty movement's goal of decoupling user data from any single vendor's application.
insights.locator: Ultimately, his vision points toward a future
